questions.questionText: The normal cardiac output at rest is approximately:
questions.explanation: Normal resting CO ≈ 5 L/min (SV ~70 mL × HR ~72 bpm). CO = SV × HR. Cardiac index = CO/BSA (normal ~3.2 L/min/m²). CO increases during exercise to ~25 L/min (trained athletes up to 35 L/min). Measured by Fick's method, thermodilution, echocardiography. CO increases with increased preload, decreased afterload, increased contractility (inotropy), and increased HR (chronotropy). Reference: Guyton — Cardiac Output.

questions.questionText: The SA node is the normal pacemaker of the heart because it has:
questions.optionB: The fastest rate of spontaneous depolarization (highest automaticity)
questions.explanation: SA node has the fastest rate of spontaneous diastolic (phase 4) depolarization → fires first → drives the heart rate (60-100 bpm). If SA node fails: AV node takes over (~40-60 bpm), then bundle of His, then Purkinje fibers (~20-40 bpm) — 'escape rhythms.' The If (funny) current (HCN channels) is the primary pacemaker current causing phase 4 depolarization. Ivabradine selectively blocks If. Reference: Guyton — Cardiac Pacemaker.

questions.questionText: The Frank-Starling law states that:
questions.optionB: Force of contraction increases with increased venous return (preload/EDV)
questions.optionC: Cardiac output decreases with increased afterload
questions.optionD: Blood pressure is determined by peripheral resistance only
questions.explanation: Frank-Starling law: the heart pumps whatever volume is returned to it. Increased venous return → increased EDV (preload) → increased sarcomere stretch → increased force of contraction → increased stroke volume. This intrinsic mechanism matches right and left ventricular output. Operates on the ascending limb of the length-tension curve. Important in: exercise, changing position, volume loading. Reference: Guyton — Frank-Starling Mechanism.

questions.questionText: The first heart sound (S1) is caused by:
questions.optionB: Closure of AV valves (mitral and tricuspid)
questions.explanation: S1: closure of AV valves (mitral M1 + tricuspid T1) at the beginning of ventricular systole. Marks the end of diastole. S2: closure of semilunar valves (aortic A2 + pulmonary P2) at the end of systole. S3: rapid ventricular filling (normal in children, pathological in adults — heart failure). S4: atrial contraction into a stiff ventricle (diastolic dysfunction, LVH). Reference: Guyton — Heart Sounds.

questions.questionText: During the cardiac cycle, the longest phase is:
questions.explanation: Diastasis (period of slow/reduced filling): the longest phase of the cardiac cycle at normal resting HR. Most ventricular filling occurs during rapid filling (passive, early diastole — ~70% of filling), not atrial systole (~20-30%). At high heart rates, diastasis shortens most (diastole is preferentially shortened), reducing filling time. Systole remains relatively constant. Reference: Guyton — Cardiac Cycle Phases.

questions.questionText: The plateau phase (Phase 2) of the ventricular action potential is due to:
questions.optionB: Ca2+ influx through L-type Ca2+ channels balanced by K+ efflux
questions.explanation: Phase 2 (plateau): inward Ca2+ current (ICa-L, through L-type Ca2+ channels) balanced by outward K+ current → membrane potential stays near 0 mV for ~200 ms. This plateau is unique to cardiac muscle and is responsible for the long refractory period (prevents tetanus). Ca2+ entry triggers CICR from SR. Phase 0: Na+ influx. Phase 3: K+ efflux (repolarization). Phase 4: resting potential. Reference: Guyton — Cardiac AP.

questions.questionText: The coronary blood flow is maximum during:
questions.explanation: Coronary flow to the LEFT ventricle is maximum during early diastole (when ventricular muscle is relaxed and extravascular compression is minimal). During systole, LV compression reduces flow (especially in the subendocardium). Right coronary flow is more uniform (lower intramural pressure). Coronary flow reserve: capacity to increase flow 4-5x above resting. Coronary flow autoregulation: constant flow over BP 60-180 mmHg. Reference: Guyton — Coronary Circulation.

questions.questionText: The JVP 'a' wave corresponds to:
questions.explanation: JVP waves: a = Atrial contraction (absent in atrial fibrillation, giant in tricuspid stenosis/pulmonary HTN). c = tricuspid bulging during isovolumetric contraction. v = venous filling while tricuspid is closed (giant in tricuspid regurgitation). x = atrial relaxation (descent). y = rapid ventricular filling after tricuspid opens (steep in constrictive pericarditis). Cannon 'a' waves in complete heart block. Reference: Guyton — JVP.

questions.questionText: Mean arterial pressure (MAP) is calculated as:
questions.explanation: MAP = DBP + 1/3(pulse pressure) = DBP + 1/3(SBP-DBP). For 120/80: MAP = 80 + 1/3(40) ≈ 93 mmHg. MAP is weighted toward DBP because diastole is longer than systole at rest. Normal MAP: 70-105 mmHg. Organ perfusion is determined by MAP, not SBP alone. MAP = CO × TPR. Autoregulation maintains constant organ blood flow over MAP 60-160 mmHg. Reference: Guyton — Blood Pressure.

questions.questionText: The baroreceptor reflex responds to a sudden increase in blood pressure by:
questions.optionB: Decreasing heart rate, vasodilation, and decreasing cardiac output
questions.explanation: Baroreceptors (in carotid sinus — CN IX, and aortic arch — CN X) detect stretch. Increased BP → increased baroreceptor firing → increased vagal output + decreased sympathetic output → decreased HR (negative chronotropy), decreased contractility, and vasodilation → BP decreases. Conversely, decreased BP → decreased firing → increased sympathetic → HR increases, vasoconstriction → BP increases. This is a SHORT-TERM regulatory mechanism. Reference: Guyton — Baroreceptor Reflex.

questions.questionText: Cardiac output is measured by the Fick principle using the formula:
questions.optionA: CO = O2 consumption / (arterial O2 - venous O2 content)
questions.explanation: Fick principle: CO = VO2 / (CaO2 - CvO2). O2 consumption (~250 mL/min) divided by arteriovenous O2 difference (~5 mL/dL). Normal: 250/(20-15) = 250/5 = 50 dL/min = 5 L/min. Requires measurement of O2 consumption (spirometry), arterial O2 (from systemic artery), and mixed venous O2 (from pulmonary artery via Swan-Ganz catheter). CO = MAP/TPR is also valid. Reference: Guyton — Fick Principle.

questions.questionText: The ECG lead that views the heart from the left lateral wall is:
questions.explanation: ECG territorial leads: Inferior (II, III, aVF) — RCA territory. Lateral (I, aVL, V5, V6) — LCx territory. Anterior (V1-V4) — LAD territory. Septal (V1, V2). Right ventricle (V4R). Posterior (V7-V9 or reciprocal changes in V1-V3). aVR views the cavity and is normally negative. Understanding lead territories is essential for localizing MI. Reference: Guyton — ECG Leads.

questions.questionText: The normal PR interval in ECG is:
questions.explanation: PR interval (start of P to start of QRS): 0.12-0.20 seconds (3-5 small squares). Represents atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay. Prolonged PR (>0.20s): first-degree AV block. Shortened PR: WPW syndrome (accessory pathway bypasses AV node — delta wave present). AV nodal delay allows atrial contraction to complete before ventricular contraction (atrial kick). Reference: Guyton — ECG Intervals.

questions.questionText: Physiological splitting of S2 occurs during:
questions.optionB: Inspiration (A2 before P2, increased venous return delays P2)
questions.explanation: Physiological splitting: during inspiration, increased venous return to right heart → prolonged RV ejection → delayed P2. Also, decreased LV filling → earlier A2. Result: A2 occurs before P2 (split widens on inspiration, narrows on expiration). Fixed splitting: ASD (no respiratory variation). Paradoxical/reversed splitting: LBBB, aortic stenosis (P2 before A2, narrows on inspiration). Reference: Guyton — Heart Sound Splitting.

questions.questionText: The Valsalva maneuver causes which hemodynamic changes in Phase II (strain)?
questions.optionA: Increased venous return and increased CO
questions.optionB: Decreased venous return, decreased CO, increased HR (reflex tachycardia)
questions.explanation: Valsalva maneuver (forced expiration against closed glottis): Phase I: brief BP increase (lung squeeze). Phase II (strain): increased intrathoracic pressure → decreased venous return → decreased CO → decreased BP → baroreflex → tachycardia + vasoconstriction. Phase III (release): brief BP drop. Phase IV (overshoot): increased venous return returns → increased CO + persistent vasoconstriction → BP overshoot → reflex bradycardia. Reference: Guyton — Valsalva.

questions.questionText: The ejection fraction (EF) is calculated as:
questions.explanation: EF = (SV/EDV) × 100 = (EDV-ESV)/EDV × 100. Normal EF: 55-70%. HFrEF (heart failure with reduced EF): <40%. HFpEF (preserved EF): ≥50%. Normal values: EDV ~120 mL, ESV ~50 mL, SV ~70 mL. EF = 70/120 ≈ 58%. EF is a key prognostic indicator in heart failure and post-MI. Measured by echocardiography, nuclear imaging, cardiac MRI. Reference: Guyton — Ejection Fraction.

questions.questionText: The blood-brain barrier is absent in the circumventricular organs. Which of the following has NO blood-brain barrier?
questions.explanation: Circumventricular organs (CVOs) lack a BBB: area postrema (chemoreceptor trigger zone), median eminence, posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis), pineal gland, subfornical organ, organum vasculosum of lamina terminalis, subcommissural organ. They need to monitor blood composition directly. Area postrema detects toxins → triggers vomiting. Reference: Guyton — Circumventricular Organs.

questions.questionText: Venous return is increased by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Venous return is DECREASED by prolonged standing (venous pooling in lower limbs → reduced preload → can cause vasovagal syncope). Venous return is increased by: skeletal muscle pump (contraction compresses veins), respiratory pump (inspiration → negative intrathoracic pressure → draws blood toward thorax), venous valves (prevent backflow), venoconstriction (sympathetic stimulation). Reference: Guyton — Venous Return.

questions.questionText: The sympathetic stimulation of the heart causes:
questions.optionA: Decreased heart rate and decreased contractility
questions.optionB: Increased HR, increased contractility, increased conduction velocity
questions.explanation: Sympathetic stimulation (via β1 receptors, norepinephrine): positive chronotropy (increased HR), positive inotropy (increased contractility), positive dromotropy (increased conduction velocity), positive lusitropy (enhanced relaxation). Also increases coronary blood flow (metabolic demand) and renin release. Parasympathetic (vagus, ACh on M2 receptors): opposite effects mainly on SA/AV nodes (negative chronotropy, dromotropy). Reference: Guyton — Cardiac Autonomic Control.

questions.questionText: The Wiggers diagram shows the relationship between:
questions.optionB: Pressure, volume, ECG, and heart sounds during the cardiac cycle
questions.explanation: The Wiggers diagram is a comprehensive graphical representation of the cardiac cycle showing simultaneous recordings of: aortic pressure, LV pressure, LA pressure, LV volume, ECG, and heart sounds. It illustrates the temporal relationships between electrical events, pressure changes, valve events, and heart sounds throughout systole and diastole. Essential for understanding cardiac physiology. Reference: Guyton — Cardiac Cycle.

questions.questionText: Autoregulation of blood flow is best developed in which organ?
questions.explanation: Autoregulation = maintenance of constant blood flow despite changes in perfusion pressure. Best developed in: brain (MAP 60-160 mmHg) and kidneys (MAP 80-180 mmHg). Also well developed in the heart. Mechanisms: myogenic (Bayliss effect — vessels constrict with increased pressure) and metabolic (vasodilator metabolites accumulate when flow is reduced). Lungs have unique HPV (hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction — opposite to systemic). Reference: Guyton — Autoregulation.

questions.questionText: The Windkessel function of the aorta refers to:
questions.optionB: Elastic recoil converting pulsatile flow to continuous flow
questions.explanation: Windkessel ('air chamber') function: the elastic aorta distends during systole (stores energy) and recoils during diastole (converts pulsatile systolic flow into more continuous peripheral flow). This dampens pulse pressure. In elderly/atherosclerosis: aortic stiffness increases → loss of Windkessel function → increased SBP, decreased DBP, widened pulse pressure, isolated systolic hypertension. Reference: Guyton — Aortic Function.

questions.questionText: Cardiac output during exercise increases mainly due to:
questions.optionB: Increased heart rate and stroke volume
questions.explanation: During exercise, CO increases up to 5-6 fold (25-35 L/min). Main mechanisms: increased HR (up to 180+ bpm, sympathetic and decreased vagal tone) and increased SV (enhanced contractility + increased venous return). Additionally: decreased TPR (vasodilation in exercising muscles), redistribution of blood flow. Initially SV increases, then plateaus — further CO increase is mainly from HR. Reference: Guyton — Exercise Cardiovascular.

questions.questionText: Pressure-volume loop of the left ventricle: increased preload shifts the loop to the:
questions.explanation: Increased preload → larger EDV → PV loop shifts RIGHT and wider (Frank-Starling mechanism → increased SV). Increased afterload → higher pressure needed for ejection, larger ESV → narrower and taller loop. Increased contractility → steeper ESPVR line → lower ESV, increased SV. Decreased contractility → flatter ESPVR → larger ESV, decreased SV. PV loops integrate preload, afterload, and contractility. Reference: Guyton — PV Loop.

questions.questionText: The Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System is activated in response to:
questions.optionB: Decreased renal perfusion, decreased Na+ at macula densa, sympathetic activation
questions.explanation: RAAS activation triggers: (1) Decreased renal perfusion (detected by juxtaglomerular cells), (2) Decreased NaCl at macula densa, (3) β1 sympathetic stimulation of JG cells. Renin → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → (ACE in lungs) → angiotensin II → vasoconstriction + aldosterone release + ADH release + Na+ reabsorption + thirst. ACE inhibitors and ARBs block this system. Reference: Guyton — RAAS.

questions.questionText: The most powerful vasodilator produced by the endothelium is:
questions.explanation: Nitric oxide (NO, previously called EDRF) is the most potent endothelial vasodilator. Produced by eNOS (endothelial NO synthase) from L-arginine. NO → activates guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation. Also inhibits platelet aggregation. Nitroglycerin works as a NO donor. Sildenafil inhibits phosphodiesterase-5 → increases cGMP → enhanced vasodilation. Endothelin-1 is the most potent endogenous vasoconstrictor. Reference: Guyton — Endothelial Factors.

questions.questionText: In the ECG, the T wave represents:
questions.explanation: ECG waves: P wave = atrial depolarization. QRS complex = ventricular depolarization. T wave = ventricular repolarization. U wave = repolarization of Purkinje fibers or papillary muscles (prominent in hypokalemia). Atrial repolarization (Ta wave) is usually hidden within the QRS complex. T wave inversion can indicate ischemia, MI, ventricular hypertrophy, or electrolyte abnormalities. Reference: Guyton — ECG Waves.

questions.questionText: Turbulent blood flow (heard as murmurs) occurs when the Reynolds number exceeds:
questions.explanation: Reynolds number (Re) predicts turbulence: Re = (velocity × diameter × density) / viscosity. Turbulent flow when Re > 2000 (critical value). Conditions causing turbulence: increased velocity (stenotic valve, high CO states), increased vessel diameter (aneurysm), decreased viscosity (anemia). Turbulent flow produces murmurs and bruits. Laminar flow is silent. Poiseuille's law governs steady laminar flow. Reference: Guyton — Hemodynamics.
